drugs.moa: Directly stimulates both muscarinic (M1-M5) and nicotinic cholinergic receptors. Resistant to hydrolysis by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase due to the carbamyl ester group, resulting in a prolonged duration of action compared to acetylcholine.
sarPoints: Carbamyl ester (-OCONH2) replaces acetyl ester of ACh, conferring resistance to cholinesterase hydrolysis
Quaternary ammonium group retained for receptor binding but limits oral absorption and BBB penetration
Dual muscarinic and nicotinic activity due to the carbamyl group maintaining structural similarity to acetylcholine
Choline backbone is essential for interaction with the anionic site of the cholinergic receptor

synthesis.description: React with trimethylamine to form choline chloride via quaternization
React choline with phosgene followed by ammonia to introduce the carbamyl group, or directly react with carbamyl chloride (ClCONH2)
Purification by recrystallization from ethanol-ether mixture

drugs.moa: Inhibits acetylcholinesterase (AChE) by carbamylating the serine hydroxyl at the enzyme active site. The carbamylated enzyme regenerates slowly (t1/2 ~15-30 min) compared to the acetylated enzyme (microseconds). This increases acetylcholine concentration at cholinergic synapses, enhancing muscarinic and nicotinic transmission.
sarPoints: Carbamyl ester group provides mechanism-based inhibition of AChE (carbamylation > acetylation in stability)
Quaternary ammonium (trimethylammonium) on the aromatic ring provides ionic interaction with the anionic site of AChE
Quaternary nitrogen prevents CNS penetration (does not cross BBB)
Meta-substitution pattern on phenyl ring optimizes geometry for AChE active site binding
Dimethylcarbamyl group is essential; the two N-methyl groups slow decarbamylation of the enzyme

drugs.moa: Competitive antagonist at muscarinic receptors (M1, M2, M3) in airway smooth muscle. Blocks acetylcholine-mediated bronchoconstriction by inhibiting M3-receptor-mediated increase in intracellular cGMP and subsequent smooth muscle contraction. The quaternary ammonium structure limits systemic absorption from the lungs.
uses: Rhinorrhea associated with common cold (nasal spray)
sarPoints: Quaternary nitrogen (N-isopropyl-N-methyl) prevents systemic absorption and CNS penetration, localizing action to airways
Tropane ring (azabicyclo[3.2.1]octane) provides the rigid scaffold for muscarinic receptor antagonism similar to atropine
Phenylacetic acid ester at C3 is essential for antimuscarinic activity
Isopropyl group on nitrogen creates the quaternary salt that limits bioavailability to <2% when inhaled
Derived structurally from atropine by N-isopropylation to quaternize the nitrogen

drugs.moa: Selective agonist at beta-2 adrenergic receptors in bronchial smooth muscle. Activates adenylyl cyclase via Gs protein, increasing intracellular cAMP, which activates protein kinase A (PKA). PKA phosphorylates myosin light chain kinase (MLCK), reducing its affinity for calcium-calmodulin complex, causing smooth muscle relaxation and bronchodilation.
uses: Hyperkalemia (IV or nebulized, drives K+ intracellularly)
sarPoints: tert-Butyl group on nitrogen provides beta-2 selectivity over beta-1 (bulky N-substituent favors beta-2)
Hydroxymethyl (-CH2OH) at meta position of the ring replaces the catechol 3-OH of catecholamines, conferring resistance to COMT metabolism
Phenolic 4-OH is essential for beta-receptor hydrogen bonding with Ser204 and Ser207
Beta-hydroxyl group on the ethanolamine side chain is critical for receptor activation; (R)-enantiomer is the active eutomer
Absence of catechol structure prevents MAO and COMT metabolism, allowing oral administration and longer duration (~4-6h)

drugs.moa: Competitively blocks both beta-1 (cardiac) and beta-2 (bronchial, vascular) adrenergic receptors. Reduces heart rate, contractility, cardiac output, and renin secretion (beta-1). Blocks beta-2-mediated bronchodilation and glycogenolysis. Also exhibits membrane-stabilizing activity (local anesthetic effect) at high concentrations. Highly lipophilic, readily crosses BBB.
sarPoints: Naphthalene ring provides high lipophilicity (LogP ~3.65) for CNS penetration and non-selectivity between beta-1 and beta-2
Oxypropanolamine side chain (-O-CH2-CHOH-CH2-NH-) is the essential pharmacophore for all beta-blockers
Secondary hydroxyl: (S)-(-) enantiomer is 100x more potent as beta-blocker than (R)-(+) enantiomer
Isopropylamine: optimal N-substituent for beta-blocking activity
No para-polar substituent on aromatic ring results in non-selective beta blockade
Ether oxygen (-O-) linker is critical for proper receptor interaction geometry

drugs.moa: Selectively blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart at therapeutic doses. Reduces heart rate (negative chronotropy), contractility (negative inotropy), conduction velocity through the AV node (negative dromotropy), and renin release from juxtaglomerular cells. Selectivity is lost at higher doses. Does not significantly cross the BBB due to hydrophilicity.
sarPoints: Para-position -CH2CONH2 (acetamide group) on the phenyl ring confers beta-1 selectivity by adding a polar group that reduces beta-2 receptor affinity
Hydrophilic character (LogP ~0.16) prevents CNS penetration, reducing central side effects
Standard oxypropanolamine pharmacophore maintained for beta-blocking activity
Isopropylamine group provides optimal beta-antagonist activity
Phenyl ring (instead of naphthalene) with para-polar substituent is the structural basis for cardioselectivity

drugs.moa: Enhances GABA-A receptor function by binding to an allosteric site on the receptor and prolonging the duration of chloride channel opening (contrast with benzodiazepines which increase frequency of opening). At higher concentrations, can directly activate the GABA-A receptor even without GABA (hence greater toxicity potential). Also blocks AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, contributing to anticonvulsant activity.
sarPoints: C5 ethyl + phenyl disubstitution is essential for CNS depressant activity (monosubstituted barbituric acid is inactive)
Phenyl group at C5 confers selective antiepileptic activity with relatively less sedation compared to two alkyl groups
C2=O (oxo) retained; slow metabolism contributes to very long half-life (80-120 hours)
Phenyl group is metabolized by CYP2C9 to para-hydroxyphenyl metabolite, which is then glucuronidated and excreted
N-H groups at N1 and N3 are unsubstituted; N-methylation would convert to a convulsant
Potent inducer of CYP450 enzymes (CYP2B6, CYP2C9, CYP3A4) causing significant drug interactions

drugs.moa: Rapidly crosses the BBB due to extreme lipophilicity (C2=S increases LogP). Enhances GABA-A receptor activity at low doses (prolonging Cl⁻ channel opening) and directly opens GABA-A chloride channels at anesthetic doses. Action terminated primarily by rapid redistribution from brain to muscle (within minutes) and then to adipose tissue, NOT by metabolism. Metabolized slowly in the liver.
uses: Induction of general anesthesia (historical first-line, now largely replaced by propofol)
Medically induced coma (cerebral protection in neurointensive care)
Lethal injection (historical use in some jurisdictions)
sarPoints: C2=S (thio substitution) is the KEY modification: dramatically increases lipophilicity for ultra-rapid BBB penetration (onset ~30 seconds)
C5 ethyl + 1-methylbutyl groups provide adequate lipophilicity and branching for fast onset
Branched alkyl chain at C5 contributes to shorter duration via redistribution
Sulfur at C2 also accelerates hepatic desulfuration back to pentobarbital (minor metabolic pathway)
Ultra-short duration (5-10 min) is due to redistribution, not metabolism; repeated doses or infusions lead to accumulation and prolonged recovery

drugs.moa: Irreversibly inhibits bacterial transpeptidase (PBP, penicillin-binding protein) by acylating the active site serine residue. This prevents cross-linking of peptidoglycan chains in the bacterial cell wall (blocks the D-Ala-D-Ala transpeptidation step). The weakened cell wall cannot withstand osmotic pressure, leading to bacterial lysis. Bactericidal against actively dividing organisms.
uses: Upper and lower respiratory tract infections
H. pylori eradication (triple therapy with clarithromycin and PPI)
sarPoints: Beta-lactam ring is the essential pharmacophore; ring opening = complete loss of activity
Alpha-amino group (-NH2) on the side chain phenylglycine enables penetration through Gram-negative outer membrane porins (broader spectrum than Pen G)
Para-hydroxyl on phenyl ring improves oral bioavailability (~90%) compared to ampicillin (~40%)
6-APA core (thiazolidine + beta-lactam) provides the structural basis for PBP binding
Gem-dimethyl groups on thiazolidine ring provide steric stability
Free carboxylic acid at C3 is essential for anchoring to PBP active site
Susceptible to beta-lactamases; overcome by combination with clavulanic acid (Augmentin)

drugs.moa: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in sensory nerve fibers by binding to the intracellular S6 segment of domain IV in the open/inactivated state. Prevents Na⁺ influx required for action potential propagation. Unlike other local anesthetics, benzocaine lacks a tertiary amine and is essentially unionized at all physiological pH values, making it effective only for surface/topical use.
uses: Topical anesthesia of mucous membranes (oral, pharyngeal, esophageal)
sarPoints: Ester linkage (-COO-) connects the lipophilic and the short ethyl chain; subject to ester hydrolysis
Para-amino group (-NH2) on the aromatic ring is electron-donating, essential for local anesthetic potency
ABSENCE of tertiary amine group means it cannot become ionized/protonated at physiologic pH
pKa ~2.5 (essentially neutral at body pH); remains >99% in unionized form
Because it lacks a cationic form, it cannot bind the Na+ channel from the intracellular side in the usual manner; works primarily on surface nerves
PABA (para-aminobenzoic acid) is a hydrolysis metabolite that can cause allergic reactions
Risk of methemoglobinemia especially in infants due to oxidation of hemoglobin by metabolites

drugs.moa: Reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in nerve fibers. The ionized (protonated) form binds to the intracellular portion of the Na⁺ channel in the open/inactivated state, preventing depolarization and action potential propagation. Blocks nerve fibers in order: autonomic > sensory (pain, temperature) > motor (differential blockade).
uses: Infiltration anesthesia (historical, largely replaced by lidocaine)
sarPoints: Ester linkage (-COO-) is rapidly hydrolyzed by plasma butyrylcholinesterase → short duration (~30-60 min)
Para-amino group (-NH2) on the benzoic acid aromatic ring is essential for potency (electron donation)
Diethylaminoethyl group provides the tertiary amine for ionization equilibrium (pKa 8.9)
High pKa (8.9) means only ~3% is unionized at pH 7.4, resulting in slow onset
Hydrolysis product PABA (para-aminobenzoic acid) is responsible for allergic reactions (cross-reactivity with sulfonamides)
Two-carbon intermediate chain between ester and amine is optimal for this class

drugs.drugClass: Amide-type local anesthetic / Class IB antiarrhythmic
drugs.moa: As local anesthetic: blocks voltage-gated sodium channels by binding to the intracellular S6 segment in the inactivated state, preventing Na⁺ influx and action potential propagation. As Class IB antiarrhythmic: selectively blocks inactivated Na⁺ channels in ischemic myocardium (faster binding kinetics in depolarized tissue), shortening action potential duration and effective refractory period in ventricular tissue.
uses: Local anesthesia (infiltration, nerve block, epidural, topical)
sarPoints: Amide linkage (-NHCO-) is metabolized by hepatic CYP enzymes (CYP1A2, CYP3A4), resulting in longer duration than ester-type agents
2,6-Dimethyl groups on the aniline ring sterically protect the amide bond from hydrolysis, contributing to metabolic stability
Diethylaminoacetyl group provides the ionizable tertiary amine (pKa 7.9)
pKa of 7.9 (close to physiologic pH) means ~25% is unionized at pH 7.4, enabling relatively fast onset
No PABA metabolite → extremely rare allergic reactions compared to ester-type anesthetics
Moderate lipophilicity provides good tissue penetration without excessive protein binding
The 'lido-' prefix indicates amide (contains 'i' before 'd') — a mnemonic to distinguish from ester-type anesthetics

drugs.moa: Exact mechanism incompletely understood. Enhances inhibitory neurotransmission (potentiates GABA-A and glycine receptor activity) and inhibits excitatory neurotransmission (inhibits NMDA glutamate receptors and neuronal nicotinic receptors). Also inhibits certain voltage-gated ion channels. Produces dose-dependent CNS depression: analgesia → amnesia → unconsciousness → muscle relaxation → respiratory/cardiovascular depression.
uses: General anesthesia maintenance (historical; largely replaced by sevoflurane/desflurane)
Pediatric anesthesia induction (smooth, non-irritating; historical use)
sarPoints: Halogenation of ethane reduces flammability (critical safety improvement over diethyl ether)
Fluorine atoms (-CF3) increase chemical stability and reduce toxicity compared to chloroform
Bromine increases anesthetic potency (higher blood:gas partition coefficient)
Chlorine contributes to potency but also to hepatic metabolism and toxicity
C-H bond is susceptible to oxidative metabolism by CYP2E1, producing trifluoroacetyl halide → hepatotoxicity (halothane hepatitis)
Low MAC value (0.75%) indicates high potency
High blood:gas partition coefficient (2.5) → slow induction and recovery compared to newer agents (sevoflurane 0.65, desflurane 0.42)
Not flammable or explosive at clinical concentrations

drugs.moa: Primarily blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the mesolimbic pathway, reducing positive symptoms of schizophrenia (hallucinations, delusions). Also blocks D2 in other pathways causing side effects: nigrostriatal (EPS), tuberoinfundibular (hyperprolactinemia), mesocortical (worsening negative symptoms). Additionally blocks muscarinic (M1), histamine (H1), and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors, causing anticholinergic effects, sedation, and orthostatic hypotension respectively.
uses: Nausea and vomiting (blocks D2 in CTZ)
sarPoints: Phenothiazine tricyclic core adopts butterfly conformation (~140°) essential for D2 receptor binding
C2-Cl (electron-withdrawing group) is REQUIRED at position 2 for antipsychotic activity
Three-carbon chain (-CH2CH2CH2-) between N10 and terminal amine is MANDATORY (2C chain = antihistamine only)
Aliphatic dimethylamino terminal group (-NMe2) → low potency but high sedation and anticholinergic effects
Sulfur atom in the central ring determines the non-planar conformation and electron distribution
Multiple receptor blockade (D2, M1, H1, alpha-1) explains the broad side effect profile

drugs.moa: Positive allosteric modulator at the GABA-A receptor. Binds to the benzodiazepine binding site located at the interface of alpha and gamma subunits. Increases the FREQUENCY of chloride channel opening in response to GABA binding (does not increase duration or directly open the channel). This enhances inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission throughout the CNS. Cannot produce maximal CNS depression alone (ceiling effect, unlike barbiturates).
sarPoints: 7-Cl (EWG at position 7) is essential for activity; removal eliminates anxiolytic/anticonvulsant effects
5-Phenyl ring is required for binding to the GABA-A benzodiazepine site
1-Methyl (N1-CH3) increases lipophilicity (LogP 2.82) for rapid CNS penetration
2-C=O (carbonyl at position 2) is essential for hydrogen bonding with receptor
No 3-OH group → requires Phase I metabolism (N-demethylation to nordazepam, then 3-hydroxylation to oxazepam); multiple active metabolites contribute to very long effective duration
Highly protein bound (~98% to albumin)

drugs.moa: Inhibits reuptake of both serotonin (5-HT) and norepinephrine (NE) by blocking SERT and NET transporters at the presynaptic terminal. The parent compound imipramine preferentially inhibits serotonin reuptake, while its active metabolite desipramine preferentially inhibits norepinephrine reuptake. Also blocks muscarinic (M1), histamine (H1), and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors, causing significant side effects.
sarPoints: Dibenzazepine tricyclic core: the 7-membered central ring (containing nitrogen) creates a non-planar twisted conformation that distinguishes TCAs from planar phenothiazines
The central ring nitrogen (N5) is a secondary amine bridging the two aromatic rings
Three-carbon side chain (-CH2CH2CH2-) at N5 connects to the terminal amine, similar to phenothiazines
Dimethylamino terminal group: tertiary amine preferentially blocks serotonin reuptake (5-HT > NE)
N-demethylation produces desipramine (secondary amine) which shifts selectivity to norepinephrine reuptake inhibition (NE > 5-HT)
Replacement of sulfur (phenothiazine) with -CH2CH2- bridge changes activity from antipsychotic to antidepressant
Structural similarity to phenothiazines explains shared anticholinergic, antihistaminic, and anti-alpha-1 adrenergic side effects

drugs.moa: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in the inactivated state (use-dependent blockade). Prolongs the inactivated state of Na⁺ channels, slowing recovery and reducing the ability of neurons to fire at high frequency. This stabilizes neuronal membranes and prevents the spread of seizure activity from the epileptic focus. Does not affect normal low-frequency neuronal firing. Also blocks L-type calcium channels in the heart.
uses: Epilepsy (tonic-clonic and partial seizures; NOT absence seizures)
sarPoints: Hydantoin ring (5-membered, 2 N atoms) is structurally related to barbituric acid (6-membered, 2 N atoms) but with a 5-membered ring
Two phenyl groups at C5 are essential for anticonvulsant activity (analogous to phenyl at C5 in phenobarbital)
The 5,5-diphenyl substitution pattern provides selective anticonvulsant action with minimal sedation
N-H at position 3 is important for activity; N-methylation reduces anticonvulsant activity (opposite of barbiturates where it causes convulsions)
Exhibits saturation (zero-order, Michaelis-Menten) kinetics: small dose increases cause disproportionately large plasma level changes
Highly protein-bound (~90%); monitoring free (unbound) levels is critical
Potent CYP450 inducer (CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP3A4); causes numerous drug interactions

drugs.moa: Competitively inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE, kinase II), preventing the conversion of angiotensin I to the potent vasoconstrictor angiotensin II. Also inhibits the degradation of bradykinin (a vasodilator), contributing to the hypotensive effect. Reduces aldosterone secretion, decreasing sodium and water retention. The result is decreased peripheral vascular resistance, reduced preload and afterload.
uses: Post-myocardial infarction (improves survival and ventricular remodeling)
sarPoints: Lysine analog of enalaprilat — the lysyl group replaces the alanyl group of enalaprilat
Active drug (does NOT require hepatic conversion, unlike enalapril which is a prodrug)
Dicarboxylic acid: both carboxylate groups are essential for zinc chelation and binding at the ACE active site
Phenylpropyl group mimics the phenyl group of phenylalanine in angiotensin I, fitting into the S1 pocket of ACE
Proline (pyrrolidine carboxylic acid) at C-terminal interacts with the S2' pocket of ACE
(S)-configuration at the alpha-carbon is essential for ACE inhibitory activity
The primary amine (lysine side chain) provides hydrophilicity; lisinopril is the most hydrophilic ACE inhibitor (LogP = -0.85)

drugs.moa: Competitive inhibitor of HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in the mevalonate pathway of cholesterol biosynthesis. Blocks conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonic acid. Reduced intracellular cholesterol upregulates LDL receptor expression on hepatocytes (via SREBP pathway), increasing LDL clearance from blood. Also modestly raises HDL and lowers triglycerides. Pleiotropic effects include improved endothelial function, anti-inflammatory activity, and plaque stabilization.
uses: Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease prevention (primary and secondary)
Reduction of cardiovascular events in high-risk patients
sarPoints: 3,5-Dihydroxyheptanoic acid (open-ring mevalonate analog) is the pharmacophore that mimics the HMG-CoA substrate and binds to the enzyme active site
(3R,5R)-configuration of the dihydroxy acid is essential for enzyme binding; (3S,5S) is inactive
Pyrrole ring is the central heterocycle (synthetic statin); differs from decalin ring of natural statins (lovastatin, simvastatin)
4-Fluorophenyl group enhances metabolic stability and potency
Phenylcarbamoyl group contributes to additional hydrophobic interactions with the enzyme binding domain
Isopropyl group optimizes fit in the hydrophobic binding pocket
Long half-life (~14 hours) and active metabolites (2-OH and 4-OH atorvastatin) contribute to sustained 24-hour cholesterol lowering
Metabolized by CYP3A4; caution with CYP3A4 inhibitors (risk of rhabdomyolysis)

drugs.moa: Inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), the enzyme responsible for regenerating the reduced (active) form of vitamin K. Without reduced vitamin K, hepatic gamma-carboxylation of glutamic acid residues on clotting factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X cannot occur. These undercarboxylated factors are biologically inactive (cannot bind calcium and phospholipid surfaces). Anticoagulant effect is delayed (24-72 hours) because existing carboxylated factors must be cleared first (factor VII t1/2 ~6h clears first).
uses: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) treatment and prophylaxis
sarPoints: 4-Hydroxycoumarin (2H-chromen-2-one) core is essential for VKORC1 inhibition; structurally mimics vitamin K
(S)-warfarin is 3-5x more potent than (R)-warfarin; metabolized by CYP2C9 (clinically significant polymorphism)
(R)-warfarin is metabolized by CYP1A2 and CYP3A4
3-Position substituent (1-phenylbutyl with keto group) is essential for anticoagulant potency; phenyl group provides hydrophobic interaction with VKORC1
The 4-OH group is involved in the tautomeric equilibrium (keto-enol) and contributes to the pharmacophore
Extremely narrow therapeutic index (TI ~2); requires INR monitoring (target 2-3 for most indications)
Highly protein-bound (~99% to albumin); displaced by other drugs leading to dangerous interactions
Subject to significant CYP2C9 and VKORC1 pharmacogenomic variability

drugs.moa: Selectively and competitively blocks the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, preventing angiotensin II from causing vasoconstriction, aldosterone release, sympathetic activation, and cardiac/vascular remodeling. Unlike ACE inhibitors, ARBs do not affect bradykinin metabolism (no cough or angioedema from bradykinin accumulation). Unblocked AT2 receptors may mediate additional vasodilation and anti-proliferative effects.
uses: Diabetic nephropathy (in type 2 diabetes with proteinuria)
Heart failure (when ACE inhibitors not tolerated)
Stroke prevention in hypertension with left ventricular hypertrophy
Hyperuricemia reduction (unique uricosuric property among ARBs)
sarPoints: Biphenyl tetrazole moiety mimics the C-terminal carboxylate of angiotensin II; tetrazole is a bioisostere of carboxylic acid with similar pKa (~4.5) but better bioavailability
Imidazole ring serves as the central scaffold, positioning pharmacophoric groups in correct 3D orientation for AT1 receptor binding
4-Chloro on the imidazole ring enhances potency
N-butyl group on the imidazole provides hydrophobic interaction with the AT1 receptor binding pocket

Hydroxymethyl group at position 5 of imidazole is oxidized by CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 to the active carboxylic acid metabolite (EXP-3174), which is 10-40x more potent than losartan and has a longer half-life
Losartan is a prodrug; EXP-3174 (active metabolite) provides the sustained antihypertensive effect
Unique uricosuric effect among ARBs: losartan inhibits URAT1 transporter in the renal proximal tubule, increasing uric acid excretion
